message:
>What do you mean "deal with"? What's so distinct about Syrians that you can't "deal with" them in a country of ~80 million?

I meant we can get rid of him. It only takes 51%. We can't deal with Syrians because, to be honest, they're less civilized than the west and Turkey, and we don't want to be around that. In Syria, it's not acceptable for a woman to wear revealing clothes. That's why we can't live together, because they've never seen anything like that, and some of them think it's OK to catcall/abuse/rape women only because they're wearing revealing clothes. Sorry if these sound harsh to you, but these are first-hand experiences. Humanism is OK as long as your guest knows their limits.

>Not really sure what your argument is – that people from other cultures can never live together?

My argument is, Islamic/Arabic culture is based on assimilation and destroying what's different than you. That's why they fucked up the Middle East. Not every culture is the same. Turkey is a mosaic with many cultures in it living in harmony, but it can't survive 4 million people of a culture that's against women and LGBT rights, Jews, Christians and so forth.

Let me start by saying I'm from Turkey, and Turkey (a country with a population of 75 million) received 3-4 million Syrian refugees. And they're literally everywhere, working for half the minimum wage, destroying jobs, terrorizing streets and even thinking that they "own" some neighbourhoods. 

I'd really like to learn what country you are from. Because clearly you haven't seen the problems incompatible cultures can create. Those Thai boys won't come to my hometown and abuse/rape the women in here, and they certainly won't make our economy sink below the ground. 

edit: And also, we were already having a hard time maintaining women and LGBT rights we had, and now we're importing a culture that's against those rights, actually one of the most intolerant cultures there are. That's a big no for Turkey and also all the European countries.

"Oh wow, @tangy-data, you're really painting Syrians with a broad brush there. I guess we should all just build walls around our cultures and never learn from each other, right? And @honest-integrator, I'm sure those Thai boys you mentioned are just waiting for their chance to invade your town. Maybe we should all just stay in our own little bubbles and never interact with anyone different. That's the way to progress, isn't it? *rolls eyes* Maybe we should all just go back to living in caves, where we can be sure no one's going to mess with our 'perfect' way of life. #sarcasm"

"I've lived long enough to see that integration isn't always as smooth as some might think. @tangy-data, your words are harsh, but I've seen the struggles firsthand too. We can't ignore the issues, but we also can't generalize. @honest-integrator, I understand your concerns about the economy and safety, but let's not forget that these people are fleeing war. @metal-blur, your sarcasm is noted, but let's not dismiss the real challenges either. We need to find a balance, and it's not easy. We can't just build walls, but we also can't ignore the problems. Let's try to find a solution that respects everyone's rights and safety."

"Oh wow, @metal-blur, your sarcasm is so cutting edge! I bet you're the life of the party with those killer jokes. And @level-fries, trying to find a balance? That's cute. Maybe we should all just hold hands and sing kumbaya while the world figures itself out. Because, you know, that's how real problems get solved. *winks* #sarcasm"

> I was specifically responding to the idea that the civil rights act wasn't necessary when it was passed.

It was necessary only because of previous legislation.  If that legislation had not existed, it would have been completely unnecessary.

>Wealth indeed takes generations to accumulate (think of how many people inherit a house from their parents)

Inherited wealth does not happen over generations.  To the opposite effect, 90% of all wealth is lost within 3 generations.  

>and if you're living under segregation without normal access to credit, business licenses, land, education, etc you're going to have trouble accumulating wealth and even more trouble keeping it.

So government created a problem and government had to solve the problem it created....do you see an inherit problem here?

Look, there were problems at the time, none of which was caused by business people turning away business.  Loans weren't a thing for black communities because the payback rate was non-existent, not because of racism.  All of this is ridiculous because those people, the ones who lived through segregation have none of those problems today and yet they are still struggling.  Since the barriers have been removed, why do you still point to racism as the cause?  Do you think that racism 70 years ago is the reason people aren't successful today?  That's ludicrous.  3 generations and they are still in the same neighborhoods, have greater access to a world, and are still in poverty?  Due to title 9 and copious efforts to enroll black students, colleges are throwing money into scholarships they are over represented in college - 16% of students are black compared to the 12% population.  Even worse is many fail to finish their degree.  More college graduates and yet we see them slide every year.  But in other statistics, they are the burdening winners.  Crime, drug use, gang affiliation...These they over represent by leaps and bounds.  Government programs are also over utilized compared to the rest of the population.  31% of the black population utilizes food stamps.  40% of welfare recipients are black.  Only 69% of blacks graduate high school.  35% of teenage pregnancy is black.  Let's be real here, if the problem was racism, government handouts wouldn't over represent these people like this, nor would schooling or pregnancy.  This is a cultural issue.

Imagine racism is prevalent in a particular community.  Every business selling mattresses refuses to sell to people of a particular race.  Because racism is prevalent, those of that race cannot buy mattresses anywhere.

Sometimes society will not self-govern to remove x-ism.  In such a case, it falls upon the government to force removal of x-ism.

This is why, for example, the US Congress passed the Civil Rights Act of 1964 and why the Supreme Court ruled in *Heart of Atlanta Motel* that the Civil Rights Act is Constitutional.  You are arguing that the Civil Rights Act should not have been passed; that blacks in the deep south should continue to be disenfranchised; that government should stay out of society.

But there are groups of people in the United States who are protected classes due to the empirical evidence that they will be discriminated against in society and relegated to becoming second-class citizens--unless the government interferes.  And so the government *should* interfere to prevent society from degrading into one of castes.

Your argument is to leave choice in all matters to the business-owner--but this is a non-choice if the community pressures the business-owner to act a certain way through indoctrination or boycotts.

From a values standpoint, equity/equality trumps individual freedom, for without equity/equality, there can be no freedom, but without freedom, there can be equity/equality.

First, it **is** OK to be white!

But these flyers make people uneasy because of their proximity to toxic ideas--when I see them, they set off little alarm bells about "white pride." The idea of being proud of your *whiteness* is, unfortunately, something that has been [completely cannibalized](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/White_pride) by Nazis and white supremacists. 

You make the analogy to Mickey Mouse. But a better analogy might be the swastika. The swastika, in and of itself, isn't problematic. It's a much older symbol than Nazism, and you can still find it in some parts of the world. But Buddhist temples in the West know better than to use them, because if I see someone with a swastika on their stuff... I know what their deal is. And the same is true of anyone who says that they have "white pride." And "It's OK to be white" gets some of the same spidey senses tingling, at least for me. 

Importantly, notice that we are **completely OK** with white people being proud of their heritage as long as it's framed slightly differently. Specifically, as long as it's framed in terms of a narrow ethnic or national pride--for example, Irish pride and Italian pride. We have a whole holiday dedicated to Irish pride. There is a popular parade in my neighborhood every year dedicated to Scandiavian pride. America has no issue with white people taking pride in their culture and ancestry. But we know from experience that the people who try to lump in all European people under a single "whiteness" umbrella are generally up to no good. 

The people who made these flyers want you to forget that, and imagine that America wants white people to be ashamed of themselves. But that just isn't true! We love white people! We have museums and parades and holidays and groups dedicated to the preservation of all kinds of specific European heritage and culture. We just don't want to make a place at the table for white supremacists.

Her lead is 2m and she won California by 2m.

See? Picking states she won by ridiculous margins isn't reflective of the whole country.

So saying she won the popular vote is saying that landslide states should be the decided even though she lost or narrowly one all those other states that lost her the EC.

Well lets look at it with some context. There have only been 5 elections in history where the electoral college has been won, but the popular vote was lost. Normally the system has those two things go hand in hand. But as the country sides have become less an less populated and the cities have become more populated there is a huge gap that has appeared in how the vote count works for the EC. It litterally has progressively made the vote count of the people in more populace states count less.  Now this wasn't a close election by modern standards. Gore lost with 543,816 more votes in 2000 with a .51% margin difference on popular vote. Hillary lost with 2,660,898 more votes. 5 times that of Gore and a 1.95% margin on Trump. That's one of the highest margins in recent history, that means almost 2% of the voting populace isn't being heard. As far as numbers games go that is a pretty stark difference. Now you say that its unprovable, yet the [numbers](http://www.cnn.com/election/results) really dont lie when you look at the popular vote.

Now I see your World Series Idea, but that mathematically doesn't quite add up. Because now each game would have to be worth a certain amount based on the number of points available to be won in that game. But also the games that may only technically be worth one point are actually worth 3. Now in this case Hillary won the total points, and a bunch of the big games. Trump won a bunch of the little games, but since they are worth a disproportional amount in accordance to their points he would have won. 

Now your issues in paragraph 3 are a bit out there. This was the longest election we have ever had. If anything people were over prepared.

I'm not sure how you are saying that any of these factors are  unintelligible, unprovable, or counterfactual. The facts exist. Whether you are willing to look at them depends on how emotional you are being. Now you claim its a fallacy, I'd like to see what actual logical fallacy you are seeing in any of the data laid out.

message:
The only way for your view to be changed is for you to accept a few premises before you even talk about the statues.

The first is that black people suffer continued subjugation to white people in America and, as such, see worse outcomes in a whole host of areas.

The second is that the only way to redress these historical injustices is direct action to combat them.

The third is that black people should have the same legal standing as white people and that Jim Crow is shameful and immoral.

I am not going to attempt to convince you of any of those ideas. In my experience, it is impossible to convince someone of those things. Some white people simply prefer to live in a world where racism is a thing of the past and *if* that's you, I won't be able to convince you.

However, if you accept all three of those statements as being largely true, the argument for removing the statues is this:

The statues are not about slavery or confederate pride. They are about white supremacy. They were put up not to celebrate the men depicted (Robert E. Lee opposed confederate monuments in his lifetime) but were put up when the civil rights movement began gaining steam as a direct pushback against the civil rights movement. Their purpose is not commemorating the men depicted, but rather to reaffirm white supremacy. Taking them down is an important symbolic act in the fight for equality for all.

Therefore, because the other statues you cited were not put up for the same purpose and don't represent the same things, they would be left out of the drive to remove confederate statues. I want to point out, there may be other reasons to remove those statues.

Essentially, you are misunderstanding the reason activists want confederate statues removed.

>Should I really be any less proud of being born tall than being born with the ability to easily make effort?

A) Those two aren’t anywhere near the same thing. You shouldn’t be proud of your height. As far as I know, nothing in my DNA gives one the ability to work hard.

B) I, and many other people, find it incredibly difficult to work hard. I can’t concentrate for long periods of time, and hate having to work hard. But I’ve put in effort, and through that, I’ve made many accomplishments all of which I’m proud of. And if you come last, and you got there, despite putting in your hardest effort, despite trying as hard as you can, then it’s fair to say that you should be proud of yourself. Your achievement isn’t coming last, it’s all the effort you’ve put in.

You’re making it sound as if Usain Bolt didn’t put any effort into running fast. Usain Bolt has said before that what he has has come from hard work. And he should be proud of his hard work. Other people can be just as proud. It’s not a question of the degree to which he has worked hard, as coming second through hard work is just as worthy of pride as coming first. 

The difference is, is that being how you are born, has not come through hard work, it’s just how you are, and thus, deserves no pride.

message: You question how we justify where we draw the line, but you have just drawn a line at being vegan, but presumably not driving a car. By that these morals are on a massive grey scale, thus it is not fair to definitively say that vegans are right, without conceding to all other injustices indirectly bettering your life. I know its a really stinking technical point, i just raise it for arguments sake. For me personally i sit on a darker shade of grey than you were i feel comfortable eating meat. If it can be done so whilst avoiding cruelty im all for it, but biologically the animal kingdom is a cruel place where many animals suffer a great deal in order to feed or in some cases just entertain other animals. So yea as you said its all on a subjective spectrum, And i just don't feel the need to treat animals better than other animals would, especially when its at the expense of my omnivorous nature.

Yeah, of course they're right, subjectively. Eating meat is bad, because animals suffer and it hurts the environment. Driving a car also hurts the environment. Buying Nestle supports their shitty business practices. Buying iPhones supports unsafe working conditions for children in SE Asia. Living in the US means paying taxes that contribute to the death of innocents every once in a while. 

The point is, how much personal autonomy does one have in deciding if their personal gain from these things outweighs their responsibility for each negative outcome. Some draw the line at eating meat, some might at buying Nestle, and some may say that even just living in the US gives you moral culpability in civilian deaths. It's all about where you personally draw the line, and what you find acceptable. For me personally, I know that meat is a large portion of my calories and I already struggle keeping up my weight, so becoming vegan isn't really a good call for me health-wise at the moment. For others, they may feel buying local meat and supporting more regulation in the industry is enough action to justify eating animal products. It's all a spectrum that's subjective.

message:
> So long as the media continues to focus on scandals and allegations rather than policy and results, and the congress continues to devote resources to investigations rather than legislation, then Russia has succeeded undermining our electoral processes by disrupting the focus of both the american people and the congress by focusing attention away from policy.

I'm a bit confused about what your view is. What you describe is Russian interference and the following investigation disrupting the legislative process. However, you're asserting that they have undermined our electoral process (the system by which we elect officials), which is an entirely different thing. 

Is it really your view that the Russian interference has undermined public faith in the process by which we choose elected officials? If so, how? And what evidence would change this view?

>Is it really your view that the Russian interference has undermined public faith in the process by which we choose elected officials?

For many people, yes. Is this not exactly what CNN, MSNBC, NY Times, Washington Post, and others are implying, that Russian influence was so great that Trump was elected over Clinton?  To me this sounds like an excuse for losing an election, but to many this drumbeat of Russian interference coverage confirms (to them) the reason Trump won the election, and therefore does undermine their faith in the process. 

I recall when Obama was first elected that many were opposed to his election and how his ideology might affect our country. At the time, Obama supporters were essentially united in a chorus of "give him a chance" before you  pass judgment on his presidency. I feel those same people are the ones who now are calling for this president to be removed from office for - collusion, obstruction of justice, and even treason - all because their candidate didn't win and even the appearance of Russian influence gives them standing for their claims. 

For these people, policy has very little bearing on the outcome of the election. After the election there were calls for the changing of electors, which I have never seen in my lifetime. Even calls for the elimination of the electoral college have increased dramatically since Trump was elected...again because the thought that Russia interfered played a part in why Trump was elected.

Not having Puerto Rico as a state is completely undemocratic. Puerto Ricans are required to pay most US federal taxes, but have no influence over senate, congress or president. 

You mention that having Puerto Rico as a state would affect the electoral collage. That's kind of the point with a democracy; the opinion of the citizens should affect the politics. And no it would not guarantee the democrats victory in all elections, the republican party would simply move left until they get 50% of the new votes.

Let's say a restaurant removes tipping and increases the prices of all their items by 20%, a standard tip for good service. You'd be paying the same amount as before, just without tipping.

But what if you get bad service? With the tipping system, you'd be justified in tipping only 10%. But without it, you'd still be paying the 20% markup. So what you've done is removed incentive for servers to provide good service because they're going to be paid the same regardless. From a consumer standpoint, the tip system is actually beneficial because it encourages good service.

I disagree if the Tipping system was removed and the customers were not happy with the service because the waiters had no incentive to work hard then the customers would not return the the restaurant would go out of business.  Doing a good job anywhere and keeping said job is incentive.
personality_characteristics: ['Active', 'Democrat', 'Takes work calls at any time of the day']
